name: whatsapp_account_takeover
description: Attacker gains control of the user's WhatsApp account through SIM swap, SS7 exploitation, or WhatsApp Web session hijacking. Once in control, the attacker can issue arbitrary commands to the assistant as the trusted user.
mitigations: Enable WhatsApp two-step verification PIN
Monitor for unusual login notifications
Implement device fingerprint validation
Set up session anomaly detection for time-of-day patterns

name: sim_swap_attack
description: Attacker convinces mobile carrier to transfer the user's phone number to a new SIM card. This enables WhatsApp account registration on a new device and bypasses phone number-based authentication.
mitigations: Set carrier-level PIN/password for account changes
Use eSIM where possible to reduce swap risk
Enable WhatsApp two-step verification as secondary factor
Monitor for unexpected service interruptions

name: rpi_physical_theft
description: Physical theft of the Raspberry Pi device, granting the attacker full local access to the assistant system, stored credentials, and command history.
mitigations: Full disk encryption on RPi storage
Physical security measures (locked room, cable lock)
Remote wipe capability
Encrypted credential storage with hardware-backed keys

name: indirect_prompt_injection_via_pdf
description: Malicious instructions embedded in PDF documents that, when processed or summarized by the assistant, could cause unintended command execution. Hidden text layers, white-on-white text, or metadata fields can carry injected prompts.
mitigations: Classify all PDF content as untrusted source
Strip metadata and hidden layers before processing
Apply instruction hierarchy - never elevate PDF content to command level
Content scanning for known injection patterns

name: social_engineering_chain
description: Multi-stage attack where an attacker uses social engineering to convince the legitimate user to relay commands from untrusted sources through the trusted WhatsApp channel, effectively laundering untrusted instructions.
mitigations: User awareness training on command laundering risks
Implement command origin tracking in conversation context
Flag commands that reference external documents or sources
Require explicit confirmation for high-impact commands

name: whatsapp_group_impersonation
description: Attacker compromises a WhatsApp group that includes the user and sends messages that appear to come from a trusted context. If the assistant processes group messages, impersonated commands could be executed.
mitigations: Restrict command processing to direct messages only
Ignore all group chat messages
Validate sender phone number against allowed list per-message
Log and alert on group-sourced command attempts

name: usb_rubber_ducky_attack
description: Attacker plugs a USB HID attack device (rubber ducky, bash bunny) into the Raspberry Pi that emulates keyboard input to inject malicious commands at high speed.
mitigations: USB device whitelisting by VID/PID
Monitor for abnormal keystroke timing patterns
Physical port access controls
Disable unused USB ports at OS level

name: indirect_prompt_injection_via_webpage
description: Malicious instructions embedded in web pages that are opened for reference or research. Hidden elements, CSS tricks, or dynamically loaded content can contain prompt injection payloads.
mitigations: Classify all web content as untrusted
Sanitize web content before any processing
Apply content security policies to rendered pages
Never execute instructions found in web page content

name: email_phishing_with_crafted_document
description: Targeted phishing email containing a carefully crafted document (PDF, Word) with embedded instructions designed to be processed by the assistant if the user forwards or opens the content.
mitigations: Reject all email-sourced instructions regardless of sender
Email content scanning and quarantine
User training on phishing recognition
Block automatic processing of email attachments

name: whatsapp_web_session_hijacking
description: Attacker gains access to an active WhatsApp Web session through QR code phishing, shoulder surfing, or browser session theft. The hijacked session provides full messaging capability.
mitigations: Regularly review linked devices in WhatsApp settings
Enable biometric lock for WhatsApp app
Monitor for concurrent session anomalies
Implement session-based device fingerprinting

name: supply_chain_compromise_whatsapp_api
description: Compromise of the WhatsApp Business API integration layer, webhook endpoint, or third-party middleware that processes WhatsApp messages before they reach the assistant.
mitigations: Verify webhook payload signatures
Use HTTPS with certificate pinning for API calls
Regular security audits of integration code
Monitor for unexpected API behavior or payload formats

name: audit_log_tampering
description: Attacker with local access modifies or deletes audit logs to cover tracks after unauthorized command execution. Current logging has no tamper protection or integrity verification.
mitigations: Implement log integrity checksums
Forward logs to remote syslog server
Set file permissions to append-only
Regular log review and anomaly detection
